summary: I began my journey in librarianship at Columbia University's library school in the late 1920s. It was there that I first combined my passion for books and research with a desire to serve the public. The education I received at Columbia laid the foundation for my career in library science.

Working at the American Library Association from 1930 to 1933 was a formative experience for me. During the Great Depression, I gained a broad perspective on the challenges libraries faced across the nation. This role helped me understand the profession on a larger scale and shaped my future work in librarianship.

My time at the University of Chicago's graduate library school was rigorous and deeply influential. The program enhanced my understanding of library science and prepared me for both academic and administrative roles later in my career. It was a critical step in my professional development.

In 1946, I became Dean of the School of Librarianship at the University of California, Berkeley, which was an exciting opportunity to shape library education on the West Coast. During my tenure, we expanded faculty and student numbers, introduced doctoral programs, and transitioned from certificates to Bachelor and Master of Library Science degrees. I worked closely with campus leaders like Clark Kerr to advance the school's mission and respond to postwar challenges.

I had a collaborative relationship with Clark Kerr during my time at Berkeley. As a campus leader, Kerr worked closely with me and others to support the growth and mission of the library school. Our partnership was important in navigating the challenges and opportunities of that era.

After I stepped down as Dean, Raynard S. Swank took over and focused on modernizing the school. Under his leadership, we began integrating technology like computers and automation into the curriculum and research. He also oversaw the move to South Hall and the establishment of the Institute of Library Research, which were key developments for the school.

South Hall became the new home for the library school after I stepped down as Dean. This move, overseen by Raynard S. Swank, was part of the school's modernization efforts. It provided a dedicated space that supported the expanding programs and research initiatives we had developed.

entity: Loyalty oath controversy at the University of California
summary: The loyalty oath controversy was a difficult period for the campus community. It raised serious questions about academic freedom and personal conscience, affecting many faculty and staff, including those in the library school. Navigating this challenge required sensitivity and a commitment to the values of education and integrity.
